key_insight: The paper shows that contact-induced facial wrinkles can be simulated realistically by combining viscoelastic skin mechanics, high-order solid-shell FEM, and anatomically inspired ligament constraints.
engineering_relevance: This provides a physically grounded way to predict fine-scale facial deformation under contact, useful for animation, virtual avatars, and digital human simulation.
method: Model skin as a viscoelastic layered material with time-dependent relaxation.
Use high-order prismatic solid-shell finite elements to capture through-thickness stresses and high-frequency wrinkling.
Introduce continuum-based skin ligaments to encode heterogeneous attachments and mobility constraints.
evidence_signal: The method is evaluated on synthetic examples and compared against real-world footage, showing temporally coherent and visually realistic wrinkle patterns during transient contact.
watch_item: The approach likely requires careful calibration of viscoelastic and ligament parameters, and high-order FEM may be computationally demanding for practical use.
paper_title: Physics-Based Simulation of Contact-Induced Facial Wrinkling
